skill.name: python-executor
skill.description: Run Python code through the inference.sh hosted sandbox for data analysis, scraping, media processing, file generation, and automation tasks.
skill.summary: Execute Python scripts in a hosted sandbox with common data, web, media, and document libraries.
skill.author: inference-sh-9

security_audit.summary: The static external-command results are mostly false positives from Markdown code fences, inline package names, and CLI examples. The pipe-to-shell quick start is a confirmed high-risk supply-chain pattern, while the network examples are intentional capabilities for scraping and API calls. No prompt injection text or confirmed malicious intent was found, so this is not a critical block.

high_findings.title: Remote Install Script Piped to Shell
high_findings.description: The quick start recommends `curl -fsSL https://cli.inference.sh | sh && infsh login`. Piping a remote script directly into a shell can execute changed or compromised installer content before review. The later checksum note does not remove this risk because the repository does not include the installer logic for local verification.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact pipe-to-shell command is visible in the quick start. This is a well-known supply-chain execution risk even when the service itself may be legitimate.

medium_findings.title: Hosted Arbitrary Python Execution Capability
medium_findings.description: The skill is designed to send user-provided Python code to the `infsh/python-executor` app. This is the core feature, but it can process sensitive data and produce files, so users need clear trust boundaries for the hosted sandbox.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The input schema and examples show arbitrary Python code supplied as input and output files returned from `outputs/`. Legitimate sandbox execution is possible, but data exposure risk remains.

medium_findings.title: Network-Enabled Scraping and API Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill documents installed HTTP clients and includes examples that fetch web pages and call external APIs. This appears intentional and useful, but it can transmit user data to third-party services if prompts include secrets or private content.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network libraries and request examples are explicit. They are not inherently malicious, but they create a real exfiltration path when users run unreviewed code.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Ruby Execution
low_findings.description: Most static external-command hits come from fenced Markdown examples and inline package names, not Ruby backtick execution. These examples still show CLI use, but the Ruby-specific finding is a false positive.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding lines are Markdown code fences and shell examples in SKILL.md. No Ruby source file or Ruby backtick operator is present.

low_findings.title: Documentation URLs and Image Link
low_findings.description: Several hardcoded URLs point to the service site, a hosted image, documentation, and a checksum page. These links are expected documentation content, not hidden callbacks or credential exfiltration code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible in descriptive text and Markdown links. No evidence found that these links automatically transmit secrets from the user environment.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Pattern Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm hit at the description line appears to be a pattern collision with service text or the `.sh` domain. The only explicit cryptographic statement is SHA-256 checksum verification in the install note.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: No MD5, SHA-1, DES, or similar weak algorithm appears in the reviewed context. The file instead mentions SHA-256 verification on line 24.

low_findings.title: Network Reconnaissance Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The network reconnaissance hit maps to the safe-execution note and nearby general documentation, not scanning code. No evidence found of port scanning, host discovery, or probing logic in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines contain user-facing notes about CPU-only execution, isolated subprocesses, plotting, and output files. They do not contain reconnaissance commands or code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe Remote Installer to Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: A remote installer URL is piped directly to `sh`, which executes downloaded content without requiring the user to inspect it first.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The line contains the exact `curl ... | sh` pattern. The risk is direct remote code execution during installation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Agent-Accessible External CLI Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill allows `Bash(infsh *)` and centers execution on an external CLI. The restriction to `infsh` narrows the command surface, but the CLI still sends code and data to a hosted service.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools line and examples show agent-accessible CLI execution. The command prefix restriction reduces command injection risk but does not eliminate hosted execution risk.

content.user_title: Run Python Scripts in a Hosted Sandbox
content.value_statement: Teams often need Python execution without managing local environments. This skill runs scripts through inference.sh with common libraries already installed.
actual_capabilities: Runs Python snippets through the `infsh/python-executor` hosted app.
Supports common data libraries, including NumPy, Pandas, SciPy, Matplotlib, Seaborn, and Plotly.
Documents web scraping and API workflows with requests, BeautifulSoup, Selenium, Playwright, and Scrapy.
Creates returned output files from the `outputs/` directory, including charts, CSV files, videos, PDFs, and 3D models.
Offers default and high-memory variants with documented RAM and timeout limits.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI and login before use.
Runs in a CPU-only environment without GPU or dedicated machine learning libraries.
Supports non-interactive scripts, so graphical output should be saved to files.
Executes code on a hosted service, so sensitive data needs careful handling.

use_cases.title: Analyze Small Business Data
use_cases.description: Load tabular data, calculate summaries, and generate charts without configuring a Python environment.

use_cases.title: Prototype Web Data Collection
use_cases.description: Fetch public pages or APIs, parse structured information, and return clean files for review.

use_cases.title: Generate Media and Documents
use_cases.description: Create images, videos, PDFs, and 3D files from scripted workflows inside the hosted sandbox.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Simple Calculation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use python-executor to run a short Python script that calculates summary statistics for this list: [12, 15, 19, 21]. Return the printed result.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Chart File
prompt_templates.prompt: Use python-executor to create a bar chart from these monthly totals: January 120, February 160, March 140. Save the chart in the outputs folder.

prompt_templates.title: Scrape a Public Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Use python-executor to fetch a public web page I provide, extract the page title and main headings, and return a clean text summary.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Multi-Step Report
prompt_templates.prompt: Use python-executor to load my CSV data, clean missing values, produce a summary table, create two charts, and save a PDF report in outputs.

output_examples.input: Analyze a small sales table and create a chart.
output_examples.output: Returns a concise sales summary and a chart image file saved from the hosted Python run.

output_examples.input: Extract information from a public web page.
output_examples.output: Returns the page title, selected headings, and a plain-language extraction summary.

output_examples.input: Generate a short video asset from scripted instructions.
output_examples.output: Returns a rendered video file and a brief note with the duration and export settings.

best_practices: Review generated Python code before execution, especially when it includes network calls.
Save charts and generated assets into the `outputs/` directory so they are returned.
Use short timeouts for simple tasks and the high-memory variant only for large data jobs.
anti_patterns: Do not send secrets, private customer data, or credentials to the hosted executor.
Do not pipe remote installers into a shell without independent verification.
Do not use interactive display calls when a saved output file is required.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It runs Python code through the inference.sh hosted Python executor and can return generated files.

faq.question: Does it run Python locally?
faq.answer: No. The documented workflow uses the `infsh` CLI to send code to a hosted sandbox.

faq.question: Can it access the internet?
faq.answer: The examples include HTTP clients and API calls, so users should treat network access as part of the capability.

faq.question: Can it create files?
faq.answer: Yes. Files written to the `outputs/` directory are documented as automatically returned.

faq.question: Is it safe for sensitive data?
faq.answer: Use caution. Hosted code execution and network-capable scripts can expose sensitive data if prompts or code include it.

faq.question: Why is publication not recommended?
faq.answer: The quick start recommends piping a remote installer into `sh`, which is a high-risk supply-chain pattern.
